channel.title: CyberKareem security writing
channel.description: Security research, engineering notes, certification reviews, and authorized-lab walkthroughs by Abdullah Kareem.

item.title: CVE-2026-16532: Link Library's Public Submission Form Reached Raw SQL
item.description: Link Library concatenated a URL from its public submission form into a duplicate-check query, creating unauthenticated blind SQL injection before 7.9.3.

item.title: Building kameHunt: LLM-Assisted Variant Analysis for CVE Research
item.description: kameHunt hunts the shape of a known bug across public source code, then validates and ranks the matches so a human reviews a short list of leads instead of pattern-matcher noise. Every result is an unverified lead.

item.title: CVE-2026-63007: Two Uyuni Formula Methods Skipped the Cross-Org Check
item.description: Two public formula XML-RPC methods in Uyuni resolved a server group or a set of servers by id and omitted the organization and permission check that every sibling method enforces. Any authenticated user could read and tamper with formula configuration belonging to another organization.

item.title: CVE-2026-62666: A User Manager Could Mint a Super-Admin API Key
item.description: A delegated user manager could create an API key for a super-admin account. The key inherited the target account's authority, turning a limited role into full instance control.

item.title: CVE-2026-62670: Missing Flex Permissions Failed Open
item.description: The Grav Flex Objects API treated a missing directory permissions map as permission to proceed. An account with only api.access could operate on a directory it was not authorized to list.

item.title: CVE-2026-62671: A Cross-Site GET Could Rotate a Grav TOTP Secret
item.description: A login task regenerated and saved the active user's TOTP secret without a CSRF nonce. Under Grav's default SameSite=Lax setting, an off-site top-level GET could desynchronize a logged-in user's authenticator.

item.title: CVE-2026-55675: Fork PR Code Ran in a Privileged GitHub Actions Job
item.description: Untrusted pull-request code reached a CI job holding deployment secrets. Separating build from deploy removed that path.

item.title: CVE-2026-55890: Grav's style() Method Bypassed the Earlier Fix
item.description: Grav blocked style through attribute(), but the sibling style() action still accepted editor-controlled CSS.

item.title: CVE-2026-59185: Missing Installation Ownership Check Allowed Cross-Tenant Access
item.description: The backend accepted a GitHub App installation ID without verifying that the authorizing GitHub user could access that installation.

item.title: CVE-2026-59889: @JsonUnwrapped Replay Skipped an @JsonView Check
item.description: Jackson enforced @JsonView for creator properties but skipped the check when @JsonUnwrapped replayed field- and setter-backed properties.

item.title: CVE-2026-59979: Missing Tenant Checks in Yosemite-Crew Routes
item.description: Several read, update, and delete routes either lacked authentication or stopped at identity checks before globally keyed lookups.

item.title: CVE-2026-61448: Malformed Content-Type Led to Stored XSS in Parse Server
item.description: Parse Server accepted malformed media types for unknown extensions, letting some storage adapters serve uploads that browsers could sniff as HTML.

item.title: CVE-2026-61706: Pull Request Title Injection in Infracost Actions
item.description: A pull request title was inserted directly into a Bash run block, allowing GitHub Actions expression expansion to turn untrusted text into runner commands.

item.title: CVE-2026-61826: Bazarr's Plex OAuth API Lacked Authentication
item.description: Seventeen Plex API methods lacked Bazarr's normal API-key gate, exposing persistent configuration changes and, on configured instances, token-bearing server requests.

item.title: Building seg-test: A PCI DSS Segmentation Evidence Runner
item.description: seg-test runs approved checks from defined source VLANs, preserves raw Nmap output, and packages evidence without making compliance decisions.

item.title: Hack The Box: Sizzle Walkthrough
item.description: A writable SMB share enables SCF-based NTLM capture and certificate-authenticated WinRM access, after which a recovered privileged hash leads to DCSync and Administrator access.

item.title: Hack The Box: Falafel Walkthrough
item.description: PHP type juggling bypasses the admin login and filename truncation turns an upload into a web shell, followed by reused credentials, framebuffer capture and disk-group access to the root SSH key.

item.title: Hack The Box: Hackback Walkthrough
item.description: JavaScript deobfuscation reveals a hidden panel whose PHP log inclusion enables file access, exposed credentials and an ASPX tunnel reach internal WinRM, and PrintSpoofer turns SeImpersonatePrivilege into SYSTEM.

item.title: Hack The Box: Holiday Walkthrough
item.description: SQL injection recovers a valid login, stored XSS steals the administrator session, command injection creates a web shell, and passwordless npm installation executes package lifecycle code as root.

item.title: Hack The Box: JobTwo Walkthrough
item.description: A macro document sent through the job workflow opens a user shell, hMailServer data yields a WinRM pivot, and unsafe Veeam Mount Service deserialization executes commands as SYSTEM.

item.title: Hack The Box: Obscurity Walkthrough
item.description: A leaked custom web-server source file exposed Python exec injection for a www-data shell, a known-plaintext attack recovered Robert's password, and argument injection in his sudoed BetterSSH script selected root as the execution user.

item.title: Hack The Box: Oouch Walkthrough
item.description: Two OAuth CSRF flaws hijacked the consumer admin and stole an authorization code for qtc's SSH key, then a world-writable uWSGI socket pivoted into the consumer container and DBus command injection executed as root.

item.title: Hack The Box: Overflow Walkthrough
item.description: A padding oracle forged an admin cookie, log-view SQL injection led to CMS credentials and an ExifTool upload shell, then reused config credentials and a poisoned cron download reached tester, whose setuid file-encryption bug and TOCTOU race exposed root data.

item.title: Hack The Box: Retired Walkthrough
item.description: Execute-after-redirect file reads leaked the license service binary and live memory map for a ROP foothold, a backup symlink exposed dev's SSH key, and a capable helper registered a binfmt_misc rule that inherited root from a setuid binary.

item.title: Hack The Box: Scanned Walkthrough
item.description: Two sandbox flaws exposed a host directory through another process's open file descriptor, letting forged syscall logs leak Django credentials for Clarence, then a jail race loaded a malicious PAM library through setuid su and created a root shell.

item.title: Hack The Box: Talkative Walkthrough
item.description: Unauthenticated Jamovi R execution exposed Bolt credentials, Bolt code execution and password reuse reached Saul on the host, then an unauthenticated MongoDB takeover enabled a Rocket.Chat webhook shell whose dangerous capability read the host's root flag.

item.title: Hack The Box: Vessel Walkthrough
item.description: An exposed Git repository led to a mysqljs type-confusion login bypass, an Open Web Analytics reset-token leak and log poisoning gave a shell, and a predictable password generator unlocked Ethan before the setuid pinns core-pattern flaw created a root shell.

item.title: Secure Automated Purge: Building a Bootable Disk-Sanitization Utility
item.description: A bootable disk-sanitization prototype that requires device-specific validation and review against NIST SP 800-88 Rev.2 before operational use.

item.title: What Ninety Days of Stoic Practice Changed for Me
item.description: A personal account of using Stoic practices to manage anxiety, build consistency, and carry those habits into security work.

item.title: BitLocker Cryptographic Erase Utility: Design, Limits, and Validation
item.description: A reviewed account of a BitLocker-based cryptographic-erase prototype, its operating assumptions, and the validation required before real-world use.
